design_philosophy.core_loop: Build base → Gather Tiberium resources → Create army → Destroy enemy base/units. Asymmetrical faction design where GDI emphasizes heavy strategic attacks with defensive structures and water-crossing units, while Nod uses unconventional fanatical tactics with burrowing units and deployable defensive structures.
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource scarcity (two Tiberium types) balanced against faction asymmetry; ecological collapse narrative vs. military-industrial complex preoccupation with territorial conflict rather than environmental salvation.
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Command a global military faction (either UN-backed GDI or cult-like Brotherhood of Nod) to dominate the planet during ecological catastrophe, leveraging faction-specific technological advantages.
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Deliberately paced slowly with dark atmosphere and foreboding soundtrack; presents a thematic critique of military-industrial complex focused on terrorism and power rather than solving environmental crisis. Each faction has fundamentally different unit archetypes and strategies—not mirror matchups. Optional side missions modify difficulty based on player agency.

difficulty.continues_system: Campaign mode allows players to attempt missions repeatedly; completing optional side missions reduces subsequent mission difficulty by providing unit bonuses or reducing enemy forces.
difficulty.checkpointing: Mission-based checkpoint system; campaign spans three theaters of war (North America, Europe, North Africa) with missions structured sequentially per faction.
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High—optional side missions available in most campaign missions. Completing side objectives grants difficulty modifiers (additional units, reduced enemy unit counts) for main mission, allowing players to self-regulate challenge.

progression.upgrade_system: New unit types and structures unlock via campaign progression; faction-specific tech trees (GDI heavy armor/water units; Nod burrowing/deployable units). Tiberium harvesting powers unit/structure production.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Progression through campaign missions across three theaters sequentially; each mission can introduce new unit types or structures available to that faction.
replayability_hooks: Two distinct campaign paths (GDI vs. Nod with completely different missions and narrative arcs)
Optional side missions with variable rewards enable different difficulty runs
Multiplayer and network modes with asymmetrical factions
Firestorm expansion added global war multiplayer mode and additional units

world_building_techniques: Briefing cutscenes/mission intros establishing context within Second Tiberium War (2030)
Faction-specific campaigns with divergent narratives: GDI focused on combating Kane's return and Nod surge; Nod focused on reunification and completing Kane's plans
Environmental storytelling through Tiberium spread, Ion Storms, and ecological mutation lore
Character arcs: GDI commander Michael McNeil assigned to push back Nod; Nod officer escapes GDI-controlled execution and reunites faction
Thematic setting: Earth undergoing rapid ecological collapse, but conflict driven by resource control and ideology rather than environmental solutions

faction_indicators: Global Defense Initiative (GDI): UN-backed, military-industrial complex, focus on heavy armor and strategic defense
Brotherhood of Nod: Cult-like organization, charismatic leader Kane, unconventional fanatical tactics, global ideological reach
danger_signals: Kane's return energizes Nod faction and triggers Second Tiberium War
Tiberium contamination causing mutations in humans and new alien lifeforms
Ion Storms as dynamic environmental hazard
GDI reliance on orbital space station (Philadelphia) for command/control suggests centralized power structure
environmental_storytelling: Tiberium landscape terraforming visible in game terrain and level design
Ecological mutations reflected in new unit types and weather patterns
Three distinct theaters of war (North America, Europe, North Africa) show global scope of conflict
Bridges requiring engineer repair units reinforce infrastructure fragility
Day/night cycles and dynamic lighting simulate planetary instability
